title: Figures and Captions Interview Questions
instructions: Prepare for technical interviews with these 30 questions covering semantic HTML, accessibility, best practices for <figure> and <figcaption>, and comparisons with other HTML elements.

questions.question: What is the <figure> element and when should you use it?
questions.answer: <figure> is a semantic HTML5 element for self-contained content that can be referenced as a single unit. Use it for images with captions, code snippets, diagrams, charts, pull quotes, tables, and any content that could be moved to an appendix without affecting meaning.

questions.question: What is the difference between <figure> and <figcaption>?
questions.answer: <figure> is the container for self-contained content. <figcaption> is an optional child element that provides a caption or legend for that content. A <figure> can have zero or one <figcaption>.

questions.question: Can <figcaption> appear before the content in a <figure>?
questions.answer: Yes, <figcaption> can be placed as either the first or the last child of <figure>. Placing it first is common for tables (as a table title), while placing it last is common for images (as an explanatory caption).

questions.question: What kind of content can go inside a <figure>?
questions.answer: Images, videos, audio, tables, code blocks, blockquotes, diagrams, charts, illustrations, or any combination of these. Multiple elements (e.g., several images) can share a single <figcaption>.

questions.question: Is <figcaption> required for <figure> to be valid?
questions.answer: No, <figcaption> is optional. However, including a caption typically improves accessibility and semantic clarity. A figure without a caption is still valid HTML.

questions.question: What is the implicit ARIA role of the <figure> element?
questions.answer: The implicit ARIA role is 'figure'. If the <figure> contains a <figcaption>, the browser exposes the caption as the accessible name for the figure.

questions.question: How do you make a figure with multiple images accessible?
questions.answer: Each image still needs its own alt text describing its content. The <figcaption> provides a shared caption for the group. Use aria-labelledby on the figure pointing to the figcaption if needed for explicit association.

questions.question: What is the difference between <figure> and <aside>?
questions.answer: <figure> contains self-contained content directly referenced from the main text. <aside> contains content tangentially related to the surrounding content. A figure might be essential to understanding the text, while an aside is supplementary.

questions.question: Can you nest <figure> inside an <article> or <section>?
questions.answer: Yes, <figure> can be nested inside <article>, <section>, <div>, or any flow content container. However, you cannot nest <figure> inside another <figure>.

questions.question: How do you reference a figure in the text of an article?
questions.answer: Use explicit references like 'as shown in Figure 3', 'see Table 2', or 'in Listing 1'. The numbers can be auto-generated with CSS counters or managed manually.

questions.question: What CSS properties work well for styling figures?
questions.answer: margin, padding, border, border-radius, background, box-shadow for the figure container. max-width: 100% and height: auto for media. Font-style: italic and text-align: center for figcaption. Media queries for responsive behavior.

questions.question: How do you handle print styles for figures?
questions.answer: Use break-inside: avoid (or page-break-inside: avoid) to prevent figures from splitting across pages. Set figcaption to a readable print color. Ensure images are high-enough resolution for print.

questions.question: Can a <figure> contain form elements?
questions.answer: Technically yes, but it's unusual. A form widget that is self-contained and referenced as a unit (e.g., an embedded calculator or survey widget) could be placed in a <figure>, but typically forms are not figure content.

questions.question: What is the difference between <figure> and a <div> with a class?
questions.answer: <figure> has semantic meaning that a <div> lacks. It signals to browsers, screen readers, and search engines that the enclosed content is a self-contained unit. It also has an implicit ARIA role. A <div> is a generic container with no semantic value.

questions.question: How does <figcaption> affect screen reader announcements?
questions.answer: Screen readers typically announce figure content along with the figcaption text. The association is programmatic — the figcaption becomes the accessible description or label of the figure.

questions.question: What is wrong with this code: ?
questions.answer: The blank alt attribute indicates a decorative image, but the figcaption suggests the image is meaningful. If the image is meaningful, it needs descriptive alt text. If truly decorative, consider if a figure is appropriate at all.

questions.question: How do you automatically number figures using CSS?
questions.answer: Use CSS counters: body { counter-reset: figure; } figure figcaption::before { counter-increment: figure; content: 'Figure ' counter(figure) ': '; } This auto-numbers figures in order.

questions.question: How would you implement a cross-reference within a page to a specific figure?
questions.answer: Give the <figure> an id attribute. Create a link with <a href='#figure-3'>. Optionally use JavaScript to auto-populate the figure number using CSS counter values read via getComputedStyle or a data attribute set via JS.

questions.question: What are the SEO benefits of properly using <figure> and <figcaption>?
questions.answer: Search engines better understand content relationships and structure. The caption context improves image understanding for image search. Semantic markup can improve featured snippet eligibility and rich result presentation.

questions.question: How would you handle a complex figure containing a chart with a data table backup?
questions.answer: Place the of the chart and a hidden or collapsible with the raw data inside the same

questions.question: Can you use <figure> for a group of social media embeds or tweets?
questions.answer: Yes, if the embeds are self-contained content that could be moved as a unit. Each embed could be its own figure, or multiple related embeds could share one figure with a single caption.

questions.question: What happens to figure semantics when display: flex or grid is applied?
questions.answer: The semantic meaning is unaffected. CSS display properties like flex or grid only change the visual layout, not the HTML semantics. Screen readers still announce the figure role.

questions.question: How do you create a figure gallery with lightbox functionality?
questions.answer: Wrap each image in its own <figure> with <figcaption>. Use JavaScript to overlay a lightbox div when an image is clicked. Keep the figure structure intact for accessibility, and ensure the lightbox content is accessible with keyboard navigation.

questions.question: What are the WCAG success criteria relevant to <figure> usage?
questions.answer: WCAG 1.1.1 (Non-text Content) requires alternative text for images. WCAG 1.3.1 (Info and Relationships) benefits from semantic markup. WCAG 4.1.2 (Name, Role, Value) is satisfied by the implicit figure role. The figcaption helps meet 2.4.6 (Headings and Labels).

questions.question: How would you handle a figure that contains interactive content like an embedded map?
questions.answer: Wrap the interactive embed (e.g., Google Maps iframe) in <figure> with a descriptive <figcaption>. Ensure the iframe has a title attribute for accessibility. The figure groups the interactive widget with its description.

questions.question: What is the correct way to provide a long description for a complex figure?
questions.answer: Options: (1) Use aria-describedby on the figure pointing to a detailed description paragraph. (2) Include a details/summary disclosure inside the figure. (3) Link to a separate page with the long description using a link within the figcaption.

questions.question: Can <figure> be used for audio-only content like podcasts?
questions.answer: Yes. Wrap the <audio> element in <figure> with a <figcaption> describing the episode. This associates the audio player with its metadata and is especially useful for podcast episode listings.

questions.question: How do you style figcaption differently based on whether it appears first or last?
questions.answer: Use CSS selectors: figure figcaption:first-child { ... } for top captions, figure figcaption:last-child { ... } for bottom captions. This allows different styling (e.g., bold title vs. italic description).

questions.question: What are the limitations of using <figure> for code snippets?
questions.answer: Code inside <pre><code> within <figure> lacks syntax highlighting out of the box. The <figcaption> (e.g., 'Listing 1') is not automatically numbered. For very long code blocks, the association with the caption may be less clear when scrolling.

questions.question: How do you test if your figure markup is accessible?
questions.answer: Use the Accessibility pane in browser DevTools to verify the figure role and accessible name. Test with a screen reader (NVDA, VoiceOver, JAWS) to hear how the figure and caption are announced. Validate with axe DevTools or WAVE.
